node.title: Uber | Phone Screen | LRU Cache & Number of Islands

topic.title: Uber | Phone Screen | LRU Cache & Number of Islands

post.content:
Hi all, 
Got reached out by an Uber recruiter on LinkedIn so wanted to share my experience. By then, I had solved around 50 questions. 40 Easy, 9 Medium and 1 Hard.

**Interview 1:**
I had my first Technical Phone Screen with an Engineer with a problem similar to [LRU Cache](https://leetcode.com/problems/lru-cache/) on Leetcode, but with more variations and difficulties along the way. It was also presented in a very generic manner so there were alot of questions I asked along the way. I felt that I communicated very properly and the feedback was that I even had the right algorithm but because I ran out of time (40 minutes), I could not implement the full code.

**Interview 2:**
Then, the recruiter was nice enough to take the feedback with a grain of salt and allowed me a second opportunity for a Technical Phone Screen. The second round went better than the first. It was the https://leetcode.com/problems/number-of-islands/
I was able to slowly communicate and code my way fully to the solution. I got stuck on some small parts of the DFS algorithm but was able to compile. However, since this was on Hackerrank the interviewer asked me to compile but my output wasnt correct. I tried several options to change the code but wouldnt get the exact output so there was some error in my logic. Unfortunately, time had run out. The Engineer even said "we are very close" but the time was out.
Then, a week later they **rejected**. 

I was really bummed because I did much better than my first round and thought I'd get called for onsight. Do big firms expect fully working code? I thought I had all other aspects in the interview right, but seems that this was the deciding factor? Even when practicing on leetcode, i struggle to get a perfectly working solution in time. This really upsets me. 
How can I leverage my skills sharper to complete the problem perfectly in time? I still feel I struggle alot and sometimes i dont, but thats rare. How many problems do I need to solve to get better and how to I become much better than I am now? I stuggle with DP, recursion, and non-linear DS in particular.

"Do big firms expect fully working code?"
- It depends on the problem. There are problems that are specially asked with no expectation that the candidate will be able to do it, but they want to see how well the situation is handled, and how far the candidate goes. The problem you were asked (https://leetcode.com/problems/number-of-islands/) on the second round exists here in leetcode. My guess is that in this case they did really expect a fully working code. They probably also had another problem under the sleeves to give to you after you completed the first one.

"I thought I had all other aspects in the interview right, but seems that this was the deciding factor?"
- Yep. The coding challenge is vital for you to go forward. Getting where they expect you to get is NECESSARY, it doesn't matter if you excel in other aspects like communication, work experience, awards, etc... unfortunately (or not?)

"Even when practicing on leetcode, i struggle to get a perfectly working solution in time. This really upsets me.
How can I leverage my skills sharper to complete the problem perfectly in time?"
- With lots of practice. Try a problem, time yourself. If you fail, look at the answer and learn (with caution) the technique used to solve it. Repeat this process on other problems.

"How many problems do I need to solve to get better and how to I become much better than I am now?"
- This is different for every individual. I had about 100 medium, 20 easy and less than 10 hard leetcode questions when I was interview by google, microsoft and a hot local startup. I was able to make it to the onsites, but I didn't get an offer from any of them. But I know people that were able to land a job with less practice on leetcode. The best advice I heard was: keep going, keep practicing, don't give up. This way you will always be working to increase the chances of landing a job. Or, as many prefer to state (including me): decrease the chances of not landing a job.

Good luck, man. I hope we both seek what we want.

IMHO: The point where you lost the interview,  "I tried several options to change the code but wouldn\'t get the exact output". Next time do not type unless you are confident in the output. If you are not able to get the exact output but you were trying hit-n-trial to get an answer is red flag to the interviewer.

Yes. Ideally you are expected to deliver a fully working code. If you are solving on leetcode, make sure you have a policy that you don't submit unless you are absolutely sure that your solution is right (both algorithm wise and implementation wise). Basically 99% of the time you spend on a problem should be spent in thinking about efficient algorithm and once you crack the algorithm, the next significant fraction of the time should be spent on designing your code. Once you can see a working code in your head, the process of coding should be just typing and it shouldn't take more than 10-15 mins. Many people almost never design code in their head precisely. This leads to being unsure while you are typing and trying to write some hacky code for the parts you are not sure. This will lead to dirty looking code which will not work always. 
  
The other bad practice many programmers have is debugging code by running example test cases and see where they are going wrong. In case something fails, try to break your code by thinking of possible scenarios/test cases where it might not work. This should happen in your head, not by running testcases on the platform. This will help you develop the ability to see mistakes in your code before you code it. 

Note: While testing your code properly before pushing to production is a good practice in real industry. You are expected to write a bug free code in the first place, and since no matter how much of a perfect coder you are you might miss something once in a while. Unit tests and functional tests find out what you have missed in these "once in a while" scenarios. Test cases should not be used as a way construct a working code.

Don\'t worry or get bogged down on rejection it happens to everyone. You will have your day, just keep practising. Asking very complicated ones in phone interview is not a good thing but i guess that is their way.
Could you describe the LRU variation in little more detail..I mean the kind of constraints imposed and other things which differed from classic one

Hi there, I just found out about this website and I\'m blown away by how much people love to share. Thank you for sharing, it truly means a lot. What I will say to you is to keep pushing and go back to the basics, write down each graph algorithm on a notebook so you can easily refer back to it.  Once again thank you soo much for sharing.

*"How many problems do I need to solve to get better and how to I become much better than I am now?"*\n\nIt\'s not a matter of how many problems you need to solve, it\'s a matter of **time**, **consistent practice** and **patience**.
you aren\'t practice enough. the normal amount would be at lease 200 medium questions. otherwise even you could pass phone screen you will not pass onsite.

Ultimately I think you just haven\'t practiced enough. Once you\'ve done like 100 Medium questions, you would be able to write executable code for medium questions like this in the span of an interview.

I had Uber freight full stack engineer phone screen last week and he asked me to write java script code to validate sudoku board..code worked for all test cases...Interviewer pointed me there was a bug in my code and I fixed it and at the end he said HR will get back with next steps..but bit disappointed as I did not get onsite call..I guess interviewer pointing a bug is a red flag in phone screen..

Solve more medium-hard problems, that\'s what companies ask more. usually they expect working code of medium problems and they check your approach in hard level problems.\nI recently interviewed with one company, they asked a very similar question to Number Of Islands (it had 8 coordinates to traverse). They expected the full working code of the problem.\nGood luck with your further interviewing :)
Hey! Thanks for sharing your experience. Can you let us know what special do we need to have on our LinkedIn profile for recruiters to reach out to us there?
Following this discussion. I don\'t have enough experience yet to say myself...
